labacts.name: Division Algorithm
labacts.description: Computes the quotient and remainder for two integers using the division algorithm. Given integers a and b, finds q and r such that a = b(q) + r.
labacts.discussion: The Division Algorithm is a fundamental theorem in number theory. Given any integer a (dividend) and non-zero integer b (divisor), there exist unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = b·q + r, where 0 ≤ r < |b|. This guarantees every integer division produces a well-defined quotient and remainder — the backbone of modular arithmetic, GCD computation, and cryptography.

labacts.name: Euclidean Algorithm
labacts.description: Finds the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) and Least Common Multiple (LCM) of two positive integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
labacts.discussion: The Euclidean Algorithm is one of the oldest known algorithms, described by Euclid around 300 BCE. It computes the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two non-negative integers (with at least one non-zero) by repeatedly replacing the pair (a, b) with (b, r) where r = a mod b, until r = 0. The last non-zero remainder is the GCD. The LCM is then derived using the identity: LCM(a, b) = |a · b| / GCD(a, b).

labacts.name: Collatz Sequence
labacts.description: Finds all terms of the Collatz sequence starting from a given positive odd integer. The sequence follows: if odd → 3n+1, if even → n/2, until reaching 1.
labacts.discussion: The Collatz Sequence (also called the 3n+1 problem) is an unsolved conjecture in mathematics. Starting from any positive integer n: if n is odd, the next term is 3n + 1; if n is even, the next term is n / 2. The conjecture states that no matter what positive integer you start with, the sequence will always eventually reach 1. Despite its simple rules, no general proof has been found.

labacts.name: Palindrome Checker
labacts.description: Checks whether a given string is a palindrome. Ignores spaces, punctuation, and capitalization during the check.
labacts.discussion: A palindrome is a word, phrase, or sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. The checker works by normalizing the input — stripping spaces, punctuation, and converting to lowercase — then comparing the cleaned string against its reverse. If they match, the input is a palindrome. Classic examples include 'racecar', 'madam', and the phrase 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'.

inputs.label: String
inputs.placeholder: e.g. A man a plan a canal Panama

labacts.name: Recursion
labacts.description: Recursive sequence generators: Fibonacci, Lucas, and Tribonacci numbers.

subtabs.name: Fibonacci
subtabs.description: Generates Fibonacci numbers recursively. The sequence starts with 0, 1 and each term is the sum of the two preceding terms. Valid input: number of terms greater than 2.
subtabs.discussion: The Fibonacci sequence is defined by the recurrence F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), with base cases F(0) = 0 and F(1) = 1. Each term is the sum of the two preceding ones, producing: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21... The recursive approach directly mirrors this definition. It appears extensively in nature (flower petals, spiral shells) and is foundational in algorithm analysis.

subtabs.name: Lucas
subtabs.description: Generates Lucas numbers recursively. Similar to Fibonacci but starts with 2, 1. Each subsequent term is the sum of the two preceding terms. Valid input: number of terms greater than 2.
subtabs.discussion: The Lucas sequence follows the same recurrence as Fibonacci — L(n) = L(n-1) + L(n-2) — but uses different starting values: L(0) = 2 and L(1) = 1, producing: 2, 1, 3, 4, 7, 11, 18... Lucas numbers are closely related to Fibonacci numbers and share many of their mathematical properties. They appear in primality testing and the analysis of certain algorithms.

subtabs.name: Tribonacci
subtabs.description: Generates Tribonacci numbers recursively. Starts with 0, 0, 1 and each term is the sum of the three preceding terms. Valid input: number of terms greater than 3.
subtabs.discussion: The Tribonacci sequence extends Fibonacci by summing the three most recent terms: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + T(n-3), with base cases T(0) = 0, T(1) = 0, T(2) = 1, producing: 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 4, 7, 13, 24... The recursive implementation reflects this three-way dependency directly. Tribonacci numbers generalize Fibonacci and appear in combinatorics and tiling problems.
